plan.description: This Data Management Plan (DMP) outlines the strategies and protocols for handling data collected and used in a research project that combines quantitative and qualitative country-level statistical data for case studies and qualitative expert interviews. The project aims to support comparative case studies by integrating empirical indicators with contextual insights from subject matter experts. The DMP ensures that all data is collected, stored, managed, and shared in a manner that is ethical, secure, transparent, and aligned with institutional and legal standards. It also promotes data integrity, reproducibility, and long-term preservation.
plan.title: The Nexus between Economic Development, Social Capital and Societal Resilience in the Baltic States 1.1.1.9/LZP/1/24/136 (1.1.1.9/1/24/I/001)

description.description: This component of the project involves conducting semi-structured interviews with subject matter experts to gain qualitative insights relevant to the case studies. The interviews will complement quantitative country-level data by providing contextual understanding, expert opinion, and interpretive analysis.
description.label: Expert Interviews for Nexus-Dev_SocCap_socResil

 - uncompressed (space permitting)

section.description: In this section you have to describe any ethical or legal issues related to data you plan to publish. You especially have to consider how you prevent access to personal data or that you protect sensitive information. You also have to describe technical methods to process and access personal data or sensitive information. In order to fill this section, it is also significant to review national legislation, as well as contact ethics committee.
section.title: Ethical and legal aspects

field.textValue: societal security, societal resilience, public governance, polycentric approach, cognitive warfare, trust, economic development, populism
EuroVoc Thesaurus, European Social Survey Keywords, OECD Glossary

These controlled vocabularies and ontologies will be used to tag and describe the dataset and its variables, ensuring it is aligned with international standards and compatible with other research data. The use of these standards will also facilitate cross-dataset analysis and help connect the dataset with related datasets across different domains.
https://eur-lex.europa.eu/browse/eurovoc.html

The dataset will be made available for re-use immediately upon completion of data processing and validation, with publications expected in (estimated month and year) March 2028. It will be published in RSU Dataverse, ensuring open access without any embargo period, as no embargo is foreseen for the dataset. The dataset will be made publicly available as soon as it is ready, providing immediate access to researchers, policymakers, and other interested parties. Any updates or versions will also be published with clear versioning and changelog documentation.

The primary ethical and legal considerations in this research involve the protection of personal data and sensitive information. The project will follow GDPR guidelines for data collection, ensuring informed consent and anonymization. Ethical safeguards will be implemented to ensure participant confidentiality. There are no commercial restrictions on the data, and the project will adhere to national data protection laws. Additionally, an ethical review will be conducted to ensure compliance with research ethics standards.

textListValues.textListValue: data accompanied by informed consent statements

field.textValue: The primary institution responsible for handling all aspects of data management in this project is Rīga Stradiņš University (RSU).

The purpose of collecting qualitative data through semi-structured expert interviews is to gain in-depth contextual insights into the societal resilience and social capital dynamics within the Baltic States. These interviews will explore themes such as cognitive warfare, public governance, trust, policy implementation, and the roles of key stakeholders in shaping societal resilience. The qualitative data will complement the quantitative datasets by offering interpretive perspectives, expert evaluations, and real-world observations that are critical for case study depth and theoretical development.

This dataset will consist of primary qualitative data collected through semi-structured interviews with subject matter experts in Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. These experts may include policymakers, public governance representatives, academic researchers, civil society leaders, and other stakeholders relevant to societal resilience and cognitive warfare contexts. The interviews will aim to generate rich, narrative data on topics such as: Public trust and social capital dynamics Perceived societal vulnerabilities and strengths Influence of cognitive threats on public discourse Policy implementation practices and institutional resilience Roles of actors in shaping collective societal responses This data is not being reused from existing datasets but will be newly generated for the purpose of this project. While it will be anonymized where necessary, it represents original and non-public insights specific to the research questions of the case studies.
If necessary, activities like data processing, documentation, and publishing will be covered within the allocated post-doctoral research funding.

textListValues.textListValue: open (anyone is able to access data without restrictions)

field.textValue:
No embargo period will be applied to the dataset. The data will be made immediately available upon publication in RSU Dataverse. This will allow researchers, policymakers, and other stakeholders to access the dataset as soon as it is ready for public release.
However, if certain research outputs (e.g., academic papers) are based on this dataset and require publication, any potential embargo period will be limited to the time necessary for completing those publications. The embargo will be specified in the dataset's documentation if applicable and will not exceed the time necessary for the publication process.

This choice of license ensures that the dataset remains open and accessible to the widest audience, while giving due credit to the original creators. It also aligns with the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable), promoting maximum reuse of the data by researchers, policymakers, and other stakeholders across disciplines and sectors.

The dataset will consist of multiple files, and a clear file organization strategy will be implemented to ensure easy access, understanding, and management. This will help users navigate the dataset and understand the relationships between files.

File Organization Approach:
1. Folder Structure:
  • The dataset will be organized by interview topic or country, with subfolders for individual interviews, transcripts, and related documents (e.g., interview guides, consent forms).
  • Example structure:
/Baltic States
/Latvia
  • Interview_1.wav
  • Interview_1_transcript.txt
  • Interview_1_consent_form.pdf
/Estonia
  • Interview_2.wav
  • Interview_2_transcript.txt
  • Interview_2_consent_form.pdf
2. File Naming Conventions:
  • Files will be named systematically to include key identifiers (e.g., country, interview number, and file type). This will ensure that file names are descriptive, consistent, and easily searchable.
  • Example naming convention: Country_InterviewNumber_FileType (Examples: Latvia_Interview_1_Transcript.txt or Estonia_Interview_2_ConsentForm.pdf)
3. Versioning of Files:
  • Versions of transcripts, coding schemes, or corrected interviews will follow a versioning system (e.g., Interview_1_Transcript_v1.txt, Interview_1_Transcript_v2.txt) to track changes and maintain clarity.
4. Supporting Documentation:
  • A README file will be provided at the top level of the dataset, explaining the file organization, naming conventions, and the meaning of each document or data file. This will ensure that anyone using the dataset can easily understand its structure and the relationships between files.
This structured approach ensures that the dataset is intuitively organized, easy to navigate, and accessible for future users.
1.5 GB

Data Formats:
The dataset will primarily consist of the following open, non-proprietary formats to ensure accessibility with widely used, free software tools:
1. Audio recordings (if retained):
  • Format: WAV (preferred for archival, uncompressed format) or MP3 (if space considerations are necessary).
  • Software Tools: Can be accessed and analyzed using free software such as Audacity, VLC Media Player, or Windows Media Player.
2. Transcripts:
  1. Format: .doc, TXT or PDF/A (for preservation and long-term readability).
  • Software Tools: Can be opened and edited using free text editors like Notepad++, LibreOffice, Google Docs, or Microsoft Word. PDF/A files are accessible with any standard PDF reader (e.g., Adobe Acrobat Reader).
3. Supporting Documentation (e.g., metadata, interview guides):
  • Format: CSV or TXT for metadata, and PDF for guides.
  1. Software Tools: Easily accessible using LibreOffice Calc or Excel for CSV files, and any PDF reader for guides. Microsoft Word can also be used for handling text-based files (e.g., interview protocols).

4. Interoperability with Other Datasets:
  • The data is structured to be compatible with other qualitative datasets and can be recombined with other research efforts in the fields of social capital, governance, resilience, and policy analysis.
  • CSV and TXT formats for transcripts, metadata, and interview notes are highly interoperable with data analysis tools like R, Python (pandas), and NVivo for qualitative analysis.
5. Software Tools for Access and Use:
  • Qualitative Analysis Software: NVivo and Atlas.ti for coding and analyzing interview data, supporting import of CSV, TXT, and PDF files. R and Python for advanced data analysis, where interview transcripts (in CSV or TXT) can be analyzed for text mining and thematic analysis.
  • Text Editing and Word Processing: LibreOffice, Google Docs, Notepad++, and Microsoft Word for basic text manipulation and editing.
  • Audio Tools: audacity for further audio analysis or transcription.

This dataset may be valuable to:
  • Researchers and students in social sciences, governance, and security studies
  • Policy analysts and public officials looking to understand stakeholder views and societal dynamics
  • NGOs and think tanks focused on resilience, civic space, and social capital in the Baltic region
  • Journalists and educators seeking qualitative insights on trust, governance, and emerging threats in democratic societies
Due to the nature of qualitative data, access to the full dataset may be restricted or anonymized, depending on the sensitivity of the information and ethical commitments to interview participants.
RSU Dataverse
https://dataverse.rsu.lv/dataverse/rsu

RSU Dataverse is institutionally supported by Rīga Stradiņš University, ensuring secure long-term preservation, regular backups, and institutional resources for data management, provides data security, ensures compliance with international data standards, and is designed for long-term data curation.

Data quality assurance processes are an integral part of the project to ensure the accuracy, reliability, and consistency of the dataset. These processes will be implemented throughout the data lifecycle, from collection through to publication.

The Post-doctoral researcher, Ilze Vilka, will be primarily responsible for managing all aspects of the data lifecycle in this project. This includes data collection, documentation, storage, and publication. She will oversee both the quantitative and qualitative components of the project.

Distribution of Data Management Tasks:
  • Data Collection: the Post-doctorate will be responsible for conducting expert interviews, ensuring that they are ethically collected and fully anonymized. She will also handle the transcription of the interviews and the organization of the resulting data for analysis.
  • Data Cleaning and Processing: the Post-doctorate will oversee the cleaning and structuring of both qualitative and quantitative data, ensuring it is in a format suitable for analysis (e.g., transcribing audio files, ensuring consistency in interview transcripts, etc.).
  • Metadata Creation and Documentation: the Post-doctorate will be in charge of creating comprehensive metadata for the dataset, ensuring that it adheres to the DDI (Data Documentation Initiative) standard. She will prepare documentation, including README files, interview guides, data dictionaries, and ethical documentation (e.g., informed consent, anonymization logs). She will also standardize keywords for the dataset to improve its discoverability.
  • Data Storage and Publication:the Post-doctorate will manage the uploading and publishing of the dataset to RSU Dataverse, ensuring that the dataset is publicly available with the appropriate Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) license. She will ensure the dataset receives a DOI and is made freely accessible and citable for future use.
  • Quality Assurance: the Post-doctorate will be responsible for ensuring data quality throughout the process, including validation and cross-checking of the dataset for accuracy and completeness.
  • Ethical Considerations: the Post-doctorate will ensure that ethical guidelines in data collection and sharing are followed, with a particular focus on informed consent and confidentiality for the expert interview data.
Collaborators and Mentors: 
  • academic supervisors will provide guidance and periodic reviews to ensure that data quality, ethics, and methodology are maintained throughout the project.
  • RSU IT/Data Support Services will assist with the technical aspects of data storage and publishing on RSU Dataverse, ensuring long-term preservation of the data.
Windows Media Player, Microsoft Word, R, Power BI

To access and use the expert interview dataset, a variety of widely used software tools will be needed, depending on the type of data and the intended analysis. The following tools are necessary to ensure users can easily access, edit, and analyze the data:
1. Audio Data Access. These tools will allow users to play, review, and transcribe interview recordings (in WAV or MP3 format). For example, software tools like Windows Media Player, or Audacity (for detailed audio analysis or transcription).
2. Transcript Data Access. For example, software tools like Microsoft Word, Google Docs.
3. Metadata and Documentation Access. Any PDF reader for reviewing supporting documents, such as interview guides, ethical consent forms, and metadata documentation in PDF format.
4. Data Analysis and Integration. For example, software tool like R  (with libraries such as pandas) for advanced statistical or text analysis (e.g., for thematic analysis, text mining).
5. Data Interoperability. For example, software tools for data cleaning or visualising like Power BI, Google Sheets.

These tools are either free or widely available across platforms, ensuring that the data remains accessible and usable to a wide audience, including researchers, policymakers, and students.
The costs associated with making the data FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) have been discussed and are fully integrated into the post-doctoral research funding

This dataset will not be re-used from existing sources. The expert interviews will generate new, original qualitative data specifically tailored to the objectives of this project, focusing on the Baltic States and the context of societal resilience and cognitive warfare.
Before planning the data collection, the researcher considered the availability of relevant interview data; however, no existing datasets were identified that addressed the specific regional context, stakeholder perspectives, and thematic focus required for this research.
All data will be collected with appropriate informed consent, and any ethical, legal, or licensing requirements for future sharing or publication will be addressed in advance.

Throughout the course of the research, all data—including raw data, processed data, and analysis files—will be securely stored in accordance with institutional policies and best practices for data security and integrity. The data will be stored in multiple phases, with appropriate safeguards for sensitive information at each stage of the research process.
The chosen repository, RSU Dataverse, has all the necessary measures in place to ensure secure long-term preservation and curation of the dataset.

description.description: This project involves the collection, analysis, and storage of publicly available country-level statistical data to support a series of case studies. The data will primarily be sourced from international organizations such as the World Bank, United Nations, IMF, OECD, and national statistical offices. These datasets typically include indicators related to demographics, economics, education.
description.label: Statistics Data for Nexus-Dev-SocCap-SocResil

descriptionTemplate.label: RSU DMP Template
descriptionTemplate.description: This is the data managament plan for Riga Stradins University researchers

fieldSet.title: What is the purpose of the data collection/generation?
fieldSet.description: Give short description of the purpose of the data collection/generation and its relation to the objectives of the project. Also give short summary of the origin of the data

fieldSet.title: To whom the data might be useful ("data utility")?
fieldSet.description: Describe potential users of this dataset in the future. For example, researchers or students of your university or other research organisations. Research data can be useful also to governmental and non-governmental organisations working in the field, as well as to private sector.

section.description: In this section you state the purpose of the data collection/generation, as well as explain the relation to the objectives of the project. Give a summary of the origin of the data. Outline the data utility: to whom will it be useful!
section.title: Purpose of data

selectData.label: Select the data type from the list

labelData.label: If you chose option "Others", please specify type here

fieldSet.title: Type of data generated/collected
fieldSet.description:
Type of data depends on purpose and collection/generation methods. 
Dataset may consist of both primary and secondary data in some cases, however, secondary data is more re-usable as it has undergone processing and cleaning.

selectData.label: Select data format from the list

labelData.label: If you chose option "Others", please specify format here

fieldSet.title: Format of data generated/collected
fieldSet.description:
The choice of a preferred file format is crucial in order to ensure that your data will be readable also in the future. Some file formats are more likely to allow long-term readability than others are. Such formats are usually:
 - non-proprietary
 - open, with documented international standards
 - using standard character encoding, preferably Unicode (e.g. UTF-8)
 - uncompressed (space permitting)

fieldSet.title: Expected volume of the data
fieldSet.description: Size of data is significant when it comes to calculating necessary storage space, as well as space in the designated repository. Here you are expected to give approximate byte size of the data

labelData.label: If No, please describe, if you have considered re-using any of existing data?

fieldSet.title: Are you re-using this dataset?
fieldSet.description:
Before collection of new data, researchers shall consider possibilities to re-use already prepared data within or outside their institution, thus saving human and financial resources.
If you are planning to use other researchers’ data in your research there are a couple of things that you need to take into consideration, such as copyright of datasets, permissions provided through licenses, ethical aspects for reuse, etc.

section.description: In this section you have to explain provisions for description and documentation of your data in order to make them findable and re-usable. For that you have to outline the discoverability of data (metadata provision) and use of standard identification mechanisms (persistent and unique identifiers). You have to specify, if you plan to use any naming conventions for keywords. Will it be possible to clearly see versions of the dataset? Also very important to specify standards used for metadata creation. If there are no standards in your discipline, describe what type of metadata will be created and how. Last but not least, you have to decide what kind of documentation you will provide to your data.

section.description: In this section you have to describe any ethical or legal issues related to data you plan to publish. You especially have to consider how you prevent access to personal data or that you protect sensitive information. You also have to describe technical methods to process and access personal data or sensitive information. In order to fill this section, it is also significant to review national legislation, as well as contact ethics committee.
section.title: Ethical and legal aspects

field.textValue: societal security, societal resilience, public governance, polycentric approach, cognitive warfare, trust, economic development, populism

A description of the dataset's purpose and scope; Data collection and processing methods; Definitions of variables and indicators; Sources of data; Units of measurement; Time periods and geographic coverage; Any assumptions, limitations, or caveats
https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/ramon/

Where applicable, the dataset will incorporate standardized vocabularies and classifications to enhance interoperability and consistency across disciplines and datasets. These may include:
  • International statistical classifications (e.g., ISCED for education levels , ISCO for occupations (https://unstats.un.org/unsd/classifications/), NACE for economic activities: https://ec.europa.eu/competition/mergers/cases/index/nace_all.html)
  • Controlled terminologies used by Eurostat (https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/ramon/), the World Bank (https://databank.worldbank.org/source/world-development-indicators), and other major data providers
  • Geographical identifiers based on ISO country codes and NUTS regional codes
  • Variable naming and definitions aligned with standards from sources such as the European Social Survey (ESS) and the Worldwide Governance Indicators (WGI)
The use of such standardized vocabularies will support data integration, comparison, and reuse, particularly in cross-national and multidisciplinary research contexts.
https://databank.worldbank.org/source/world-development-indicators

The dataset will be made available for re-use upon completion of the initial data processing and validation stage, with publication planned within [March 2027, March 2028]. The dataset will be uploaded to the RSU Dataverse with no embargo period, allowing immediate open access upon publication.
If adjustments or updates are made to the dataset after its initial release (e.g., addition of new indicators or countries), updated versions will be published with proper versioning and documentation.
No embargo period is foreseen, as the data is based on publicly available secondary sources and does not involve sensitive or proprietary content.

For the statistical dataset component of this project, no significant ethical or legal issues are anticipated, as it is based entirely on publicly available secondary data from reputable sources (e.g., Eurostat, World Bank, IMF). These datasets do not involve personal or sensitive information and are typically provided under open data licenses, which permit use and redistribution with proper attribution.

Firewall: secures the network and storage systems at RSU to prevent unauthorized access to the case study data.
Passwords: strong passwords with multi-factor authentication will be required for accessing the data repository (RSU Dataverse) and any local storage systems used.
Physical Access Control: physical access to the data storage systems (whether local or cloud-based) is restricted to authorized personnel only, ensuring the security of sensitive case study files.

No sensitive personal or confidential data will be included in this dataset, so there are no requirements for anonymization or encryption at the dataset level.

Distribution of Tasks:
  1. Riga Stradiņš University (RSU):
  • Hosts and maintains the RSU Dataverse repository for long-term preservation and public access to the dataset.
  • Provides technical infrastructure and support for secure data storage, metadata management, and version control.
  • Offers guidance and oversight on data management best practices and compliance with institutional and funder policies.
The post-doctoral researcher, based at RSU, is responsible for the collection, cleaning, documentation, metadata preparation, and submission of the dataset to RSU Dataverse.

Supervisors and academic mentors at RSU contribute to quality assurance through periodic review and feedback on data documentation and integrity.
At this stage, no data handling responsibilities are assigned to other institutions.

The purpose of collecting country-level statistical data is to support the comparative case study analysis of the three Baltic States—Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania—within the framework of societal resilience and security in the context of cognitive warfare. This data will provide the empirical foundation for evaluating socio-economic characteristics, social capital, and structural barriers to trust, all of which are critical to understanding societal resilience.
The statistical data will be used to identify patterns, trends, and differences across the case study countries, contributing to the development of a robust conceptual and theoretical framework. Specifically, it will inform the construction of indicators on inclusion, vulnerable groups, social cohesion, state capacity, civic space, and economic security.
Origin of the Data:
The data will be sourced from reputable, publicly available databases maintained by international and national organizations, including but not limited to: World Bank Open Data, United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD), International Monetary Fund (IMF) Economic Outlook Database and IFS online, Eurostat, OECD, and the national statistical offices of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania.

The dataset will primarily consist of secondary quantitative data collected from publicly available and authoritative sources. The data will encompass a range of macro-level indicators, including but not limited to: Demographic statistics (population structure, migration) Economic indicators (GDP, employment rates, FDI, trade volumes) Education and social development metrics Governance and institutional quality (e.g., Worldwide Governance Indicators) Composite indices (e.g., Global Competitiveness Index, State Resilience Index) This secondary data will be curated, cleaned, and structured for analytical purposes within the project, enabling comparative case study analysis.

textListValues.textListValue: open (anyone is able to access data without restrictions)

field.textValue:
Folder organization by country (e.g., /Latvia, /Estonia, /Lithuania). Within each folder, subfolders by data type (e.g., /Quantitative_Indicators, /Governance_Indices, /SocioEconomic_Data)
 File naming conventions will follow a standardized pattern: Country_Source_IndicatorType_Year.csv Example: Latvia_Eurostat_EconomicIndicators_2021.csv
max 2210 Mb

The data formats used in this project are open, widely accessible, and compatible with standard software applications, supporting reuse and integration with other datasets. According to the Data Summary, the expected data formats include:
  • CSV (Comma-Separated Values) – a non-proprietary format ideal for structured, tabular data; compatible with R, Python (pandas), Excel, SPSS, and more.
  • TXT (UTF-8 encoded) – for plain text metadata and documentation files.
  • XLSX (Excel) – used sparingly; broadly accessible, including via LibreOffice and Google Sheets.
  • PDF – for documentation, readable on all platforms
  • .R – for R scripts used in data processing, cleaning, and analysis. These scripts enhance transparency and reproducibility and can be run in open-source environments like RStudio.
All selected formats ensure compatibility with widely used and/or free software, and enable the recombination of data with other datasets for comparative, longitudinal, or multi-source analyses.

The statistical dataset generated and compiled for this project will be valuable to a wide range of potential users. Primarily, it will support researchers and students in the fields of political science, sociology, public governance, and economics—particularly those focusing on the Baltic region, societal resilience, cognitive warfare, or social capital.
Beyond academia, the dataset may be of interest to governmental bodies in the Baltic States and broader EU institutions engaged in policy planning and resilience-building strategies. Non-governmental organizations (NGOs), especially those involved in civic engagement, democratic governance, and social cohesion, may find the data useful for program development and advocacy work.
Additionally, private sector actors—particularly those in policy consultancy, strategic communication, or social impact sectors—may benefit from access to the insights derived from this data to inform risk assessments and stakeholder engagement strategies.
RSU Dataverse
https://dataverse.rsu.lv/dataverse/rsu

RSU Dataverse is institutionally supported by Rīga Stradiņš University, ensuring secure long-term preservation, regular backups, and institutional resources for data management, provides data security, ensures compliance with international data standards, and is designed for long-term data curation.

Data Quality Assurance Processes:
The project includes clearly defined data quality assurance (QA) processes to ensure the accuracy, reliability, and consistency of the dataset. These QA procedures will be applied throughout the data lifecycle and include the following:
1. Source Validation: all data will be collected from reputable, authoritative sources such as Eurostat, the World Bank, IMF, and national statistical offices. Cross-referencing across multiple sources will be used where possible to validate consistency.
2. Data Cleaning and Verification: the post-doctoral researcher will perform thorough data cleaning, including handling missing values, standardizing formats, and verifying calculations. R scripts used for processing will be versioned and documented to ensure transparency and reproducibility.
3. Metadata and Documentation Checks: all datasets will be accompanied by detailed metadata and documentation, reviewed for completeness and clarity before publication. Standard file naming conventions and structured directories will support consistency.
4. Supervisory Review: supervisors and academic mentors will periodically review data outputs, metadata, and scripts to ensure adherence to academic and ethical standards.
5. Final Review Before Publication: A final check will be conducted before dataset publication in RSU Dataverse, including format verification, metadata completeness, and licensing accuracy.
These measures are designed to ensure that the dataset is trustworthy, transparent, and usable for future researchers and stakeholders.

Responsibility for Data Management.
The post-doctoral researcher is the primary person responsible for data management throughout the project. This includes the entire data lifecycle from collection and organization to documentation, storage, and publication.

Distribution of Data Management Tasks:
1. Post-doctoral Researcher (RSU):
  • Collection of quantitative data from public sources (e.g., Eurostat, World Bank, IMF)
  • Structuring and cleaning of data for analysis
  • Preparation of metadata, documentation, and codebooks
  • Development and organization of R scripts for data processing
  • Uploading and publishing data to RSU Dataverse with correct licensing and metadata
  • Maintaining version control and addressing any future data updates
2. Academic Supervisors and Mentors (RSU and regional co-mentors):
  • Provide feedback and review on the structure, quality, and consistency of data and documentation
  • Support in ensuring alignment with ethical standards, scientific best practices, and FAIR principles
3. RSU Data Services/IT Support (if needed):
  • Offer technical support for RSU Dataverse platform use
  • Ensure secure and sustainable data hosting and long-term preservation

The dataset will be provided in standard, open formats that can be accessed using widely available and often free software tools. No proprietary or specialized software is required for basic access and analysis. The following tools are recommended:
  • Spreadsheet software (e.g., Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets) – for viewing and basic manipulation of .csv and .xlsx files.
  • R and RStudio – for advanced statistical analysis, visualization, and reproducible workflows using the included .R scripts.
  • Text editors (e.g., Notepad++, or any UTF-8 compatible editor) – for reading .txt documentation files and script code.
  • PDF reader (e.g., Adobe Acrobat Reader, browser-based viewers) – for viewing supporting documents in .pdf format.
All tools listed are free or open-source (or have freely available versions) and are cross-platform, ensuring that the data is accessible to a wide range of users without significant technical barriers.

No additional funding sources are currently anticipated or required for these activities. All tasks related to data management are integrated into the researcher’s work plan and covered under the existing project budget.
Importantly, long-term preservation costs are covered by RSU, as the data will be stored and maintained in the RSU Dataverse repository, which ensures sustainability, version control, and access management at no additional cost to the project.

During the research phase, all data—including raw, processed, and analyzed files—will be stored using secure storage solutions provided by Riga Stradiņš University (RSU). The storage strategy ensures data safety, integrity, and compliance with institutional and ethical standards throughout all phases of the project. RSU Nextcloud as example.

RSU Dataverse is fully appropriate for the long-term preservation and curation of research data. It meets the necessary standards for data security, sustainability, and accessibility.
RSU Dataverse ensures:
  • Persistent identifiers (DOIs) for reliable citation and long-term access
  • Regular backups and secure storage infrastructure
  • Metadata standardization (e.g., DDI) for discoverability and interoperability
  • Version control and change tracking
  • Open access support with customizable licensing (e.g., Creative Commons)
As an institutionally supported repository, it offers a sustainable and trusted environment for preserving datasets beyond the lifecycle of the project, making it an ideal choice for ensuring that data remains accessible, reusable, and well-documented over time.

description.description: This dataset includes qualitative data derived from national and international policy documents, institutional reports, and reputable news sources. The aim is to provide a comprehensive contextual backdrop for each case study by examining policy decisions, implementation strategies, institutional perspectives, and media coverage. Sources are selected based on relevance, credibility, and publication date. Reports from NGOs, think tanks, and international organizations are also included to capture a broader range of viewpoints. All documents are thematically analyzed and systematically catalogued to support cross-case comparison and triangulation with statistical and interview data.
description.label: Case Study Data: Policy Documents, Reports & News Analysis

section.description: In this section you have to explain provisions for description and documentation of your data in order to make them findable and re-usable. For that you have to outline the discoverability of data (metadata provision) and use of standard identification mechanisms (persistent and unique identifiers). You have to specify, if you plan to use any naming conventions for keywords. Will it be possible to clearly see versions of the dataset? Also very important to specify standards used for metadata creation. If there are no standards in your discipline, describe what type of metadata will be created and how. Last but not least, you have to decide what kind of documentation you will provide to your data.
section.title: Metadata and documentation

section.description: In this section you have to describe how you will handle your data in order to make them interoperable and re-usable.  To make data more accessible and re-usable, you also have to describe how the data will be made available by specifying methods and software tools needed to access and use the data. Significant part is to explain how you will describe the data, will you use any standard vocabulary/taxonomy/ontology.

fieldSet.title: Will there be an embargo period for access to these data?
fieldSet.description: Embargo period shall be applied only in cases when there are certain research outputs based on this dataset to be published. Thus embargo period shall not exceed time appropriate for publishing the respective research outputs.

fieldSet.title: Will you provide reference to the dataset in scientific publications and other research outputs?
fieldSet.description: It is significant to provide link to the dataset, as well as data availability statement (if applicable) in research outputs such as scientific publications. In more and more journals it is already mandatory to provide a link to dataset in a trusted repository. It also gives more validity and trustworthiness to the publication. Otherwise, it is possible that public dataset will gain new collaborations

section.description: In this section you have to describe any ethical or legal issues related to data you plan to publish. You especially have to consider how you prevent access to personal data or that you protect sensitive information. You also have to describe technical methods to process and access personal data or sensitive information. In order to fill this section, it is also significant to review national legislation, as well as contact ethics committee.
section.title: Ethical and legal aspects

field.textValue: societal security, societal resilience, public governance, polycentric approach, cognitive warfare, trust, economic development, populism
EuroVoc, ESS Keywords, and OECD Glossary

As in other DMP parts the standard vocabularies and taxonomies will be used to ensure interoperability. Keywords may be based on EuroVoc, ESS Keywords, and OECD Glossary to maintain consistency and improve data discoverability and reuse.
There will be no embargo period, ensuring immediate access.
Ethical Review: The project will be reviewed and approved by the RSU Ethics Committee to ensure all data collection and sharing practices comply with ethical guidelines.

• Firewall: secures the network and storage systems at RSU to prevent unauthorized access to the case study data.
  • Passwords: strong passwords with multi-factor authentication will be required for accessing the data repository (RSU Dataverse) and any local storage systems used.
  • Physical Access Control: physical access to the data storage systems (whether local or cloud-based) is restricted to authorized personnel only, ensuring the security of sensitive case study files.

Rīga Stradiņš University (RSU) is responsible for handling the data.

Task Distribution:
  • RSU will oversee data collection, storage, metadata creation, and publication.
  • RSU IT Services will support secure data storage and long-term preservation.
  • The Post-doctorate will manage data processing, analysis, and documentation.

The purpose of this data collection is to provide a comprehensive contextual backdrop for the case studies by analyzing policy documents, institutional reports, and media coverage related to societal resilience, governance, and cognitive warfare threats within the Baltic States. This qualitative data will complement the quantitative data from official statistics and expert interview data, offering a multi-dimensional perspective on the socio-political context.

The analysis of policy documents and reports will help to understand policy decisions, implementation strategies, and the media’s role in shaping public opinion on resilience and security. This data supports cross-case comparison and triangulation with other forms of data to validate findings and strengthen the overall analysis of societal resilience in the region.

Origin of the Data:
  • The data will originate from a variety of reputable sources, including: national policy documents, institutional reports from governments and international organizations, NGO publications, reports from think tanks, reputable media outlets
Data Utility:
This dataset will be useful to:
  • Researchers and academics studying topics related to governance, public policy, and resilience.
  • Policymakers and NGOs involved in security and resilience building in the Baltic region.
  • Think tanks and international organizations examining media influence and policy responses to emerging threats such as cognitive warfare.
This dataset will facilitate a better understanding of the broader policy context surrounding societal resilience, providing insights for future policy recommendations and public engagement strategies.

The dataset will consist of primary and secondary data, collected and analyzed to provide a comprehensive contextual backdrop for each case study. The data will primarily include qualitative information gathered from policy documents, institutional reports, and media sources.
The costs for data collection, metadata creation, storage in RSU Dataverse, and publishing will be covered within the project budget.

textListValues.textListValue: open (anyone is able to access data without restrictions)

field.textValue: The data will be licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 allowing for wide reuse with proper attribution. This license ensures that others can freely use, share, and adapt the data for both academic and non-academic purposes.
max 380 MB

The data formats used in this project will be open and compatible with widely used and free software applications, ensuring that the data is interoperable and can be easily recombined with other datasets. The chosen formats will promote reusability and long-term accessibility, allowing others to efficiently process, analyze, and visualize the data.

This dataset will be valuable to a wide range of stakeholders across different sectors, including:
1. Researchers and Academics:
  • Social science researchers focused on governance, resilience, policy implementation, and media influence.
  • Political scientists and sociologists studying societal resilience and public trust in the context of cognitive warfare.
  • Students and scholars working on theses, dissertations, or academic projects related to the Baltic States, societal security, and policy analysis.
2. Governmental and International Organizations:
  • Policymakers in the Baltic States and other EU countries seeking to understand societal resilience and improve policy frameworks.
  • International organizations such as the UN, EU, and NATO focusing on regional security, resilience building, and international relations.
3. Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) and Think Tanks:
  • NGOs and civil society organizations working on social capital, trust, and resilience within vulnerable populations.
  • Think tanks that analyze and provide recommendations on public policy and societal security.
4. Private Sector:
  • Private sector companies involved in media or communications who may use this dataset to understand media influence on public opinion, especially in the context of information warfare.
  • Consulting firms or data analysis companies interested in policy analysis, risk management, and strategic planning related to national security and public affairs.
5. Journalists and media analysts who aim to understand how media narratives impact societal resilience and public trust.
RSU Dataverse
https://dataverse.rsu.lv/dataverse/rsu

RSU Dataverse is institutionally supported by Rīga Stradiņš University, ensuring secure long-term preservation, regular backups, and institutional resources for data management, provides data security, ensures compliance with international data standards, and is designed for long-term data curation.
The Post-doctoral researcher will oversee validation of the data and ensure consistency across metadata and data files. Any updates will be tracked with versioning via RSU Dataverse.

Data Management Responsibility: post-doctoral researcher (Ilze Vilka) will be responsible for data management.
  • Data Management Tasks include:
  • Data Collection and Processing
  • Metadata Creation and Documentation
  • Data Storage and Publication in RSU Dataverse
  • Ethical Oversight

• For document files (PDF, Word): Adobe Acrobat Reader (for PDFs), Microsoft Word or LibreOffice Writer (for Word documents)
  • For Media Articles (HTML, TXT, PDF): web browsers (e.g., Chrome, Firefox for HTML); text editors (e.g., Notepad, LibreOffice, TextEdit for TXT files)
  • For Metadata (CSV, Excel): R or Python (for data analysis); LibreOffice Calc or Microsoft Excel (for viewing/editing CSV/Excel files)
These tools ensure the dataset is easily accessible and usable for a wide range of users.
Costs will be covered by the project budget. RSU Dataverse will cover long-term preservation costs, with no additional funding required.

This dataset will not involve re-using data that has been previously prepared by other researchers. The data collection for this research is focused on newly generated data derived from policy documents, institutional reports, and reputable media sources, which will be analyzed thematically. However, secondary data from credible external sources (e.g., NGOs, think tanks, and international organizations) may be included in the dataset. These sources will be selected based on their relevance, credibility, and timeliness, and will be incorporated into the analysis to enrich the findings. The use of secondary data will comply with the licensing and ethical guidelines provided by the original publishers.
Any third-party data used in the research will be appropriately credited, and permissions will be obtained where necessary to ensure compliance with copyright and ethical standards for data reuse.

• Storage Facility: Data will be stored on RSU's secure network drives, which are protected by encryption, firewalls, and regular backups.
  • Software for Data Analysis: Data analysis will be conducted using R or may be other relevant software for statistical and qualitative analysis. Any analysis scripts will be stored securely alongside the data.

RSU Dataverse has all the necessary measures to ensure secure long-term preservation and curation. It provides encrypted storage, regular backups, and access controls to safeguard the data. Additionally, RSU Dataverse follows international data standards for data curation, ensuring the dataset remains accessible and usable in the long term.
